---
title: "SwitchBot Thermo-Hygrometer Cross-Calibration"
date: 2026-07-04
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    html-math-method: katex
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

Consumer IoT devices to measure and record temperature and humidity have become quite cheap recently.
So I bought 7 of them.

They are all from SwitchBot, 4 of them are their standard "Meter" product [~£5 each] (one of which I bought 6.5 years prior for £25!), and the other three are their IP65 rated meters [~£10 each].

I plan to spread them out throughout my flat to measure the heat transfer.
However, it's also a good opportunity to check how consisten the measurements are between them.

So for this experiment, I placed all 7 sensors in the same location, and will check how different or consistent each sensor is compared to the rest.

# Simulated

## Temperature Over Time by Device

![](results/simulated/figures/temperature_by_device.png)

## Humidity Over Time by Device

![](results/simulated/figures/humidity_by_device.png)

## Mean Temperature Heatmap

![](results/simulated/figures/temperature_heatmap.png)

## Mean Humidity Heatmap

![](results/simulated/figures/humidity_heatmap.png)

# Actual

## Temperature Over Time by Device

![](results/actual/figures/temperature_by_device.png)

## Humidity Over Time by Device

![](results/actual/figures/humidity_by_device.png)

## Mean Temperature Heatmap

![](results/actual/figures/temperature_heatmap.png)

## Mean Humidity Heatmap

![](results/actual/figures/humidity_heatmap.png)


![](results/actual/figures/device_summary_temp_humidity_90ci.png)
The original sensor is a bit of on outlier for humidity, however these sensors are known to drift over time, with the [SHT3x datasheet](https://sensirion.com/media/documents/213E6A3B/63A5A569/Datasheet_SHT3x_DIS.pdf) quoting a long term dift of <0.25 %RH/yr.
Over 6.5 years this translates to <1.625 %RH, which would bring the sensor within the range of the others.


Visualising the individual measurements of temperature and humidity per device, we can see a negative relationship between RH and temperature.
Under approximately constant moisture content (constant dew point), this follows from Clausius-Clapeyron because saturation vapour pressure increases with temperature.
$$
\mathrm{RH}(T\,|\,T_d) = 100\times\frac{e_s(T_d)}{e_s(T)},\qquad
e_s(T) \approx 6.112\,\exp\!\left(\frac{17.67\,T}{T+243.5}\right)\;\text{hPa}
$$
So each curve on the plot is an iso-dew-point contour, rather than a single global line.
![](results/actual/figures/temp_vs_humidity_by_sensor.png)

On this plot it's also visible that the IP65 type sensors more often jump by 0.2 degrees compared to the standard meters.

![](results/actual/figures/adjacent_step_frequency_wilson95.png)


# Bayesian BSTS Model

To quantify device effects while accounting for strong temporal autocorrelation,
we fit a PyMC Bayesian structural time-series (BSTS) model with a continuous local-level trend.
Placement effects (line and position) are currently disabled by default for the single-epoch data,
because they are confounded with device when each meter occupies one fixed location.

The model is fit separately for `temp_c` and `humidity_rh` using the processed long data.

## Observation model

For observation $n$ at time index $t[n]$:

$$
y_n \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_n, \sigma_{obs})
$$

$$
\mu_n = \alpha
+ b_{d[n]}
+ \beta^{(epoch)}_{e[n]}
+ \ell_{t[n]}
$$

When placement effects are explicitly enabled for crossover data, the mean expands to

$$
\mu_n = \alpha
+ b_{d[n]}
+ \beta^{(line)}_{\ell[n]}
+ \beta^{(position)}_{p[n]}
+ \beta^{(epoch)}_{e[n]}
+ \ell_{t[n]}
$$

where:

- $b_{d[n]}$ is the per-device effect (hierarchical random effect)
- $\beta^{(line)}_{\ell[n]}$ is the optional line effect
- $\beta^{(position)}_{p[n]}$ is the optional position effect
- $\beta^{(epoch)}_{e[n]}$ is the epoch effect, included only when multiple epochs are present
- $\ell_{t[n]}$ is a shared latent local-level drift over time

## Hierarchical and structural components

### Device effect (hierarchical)

$$
\sigma_{device} \sim \text{HalfNormal}(0.5)
$$

$$
b_d = u_d - \bar u, \quad u_d \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{device})
$$

This is a centered hierarchical parameterization with a sum-to-zero constraint across devices.
The centering removes the free common-mode shift between the intercept and all device effects,
so $\alpha$ is the grand mean and device effects are interpretable as relative offsets.

### Line, position, and epoch effects

When placement effects are enabled, raw line and position effects are sampled as standard
normals and then centered to sum to zero:

$$
\tilde\beta^{(line)}_\ell \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1), \quad
\beta^{(line)}_\ell = \tilde\beta^{(line)}_\ell - \frac{1}{L}\sum_{\ell'}\tilde\beta^{(line)}_{\ell'}
$$

and similarly for position effects.

For the special case of two lines, the implementation uses a single symmetric contrast
$\delta_{line}$ and sets the two line effects to $+\delta_{line}$ and $-\delta_{line}$.

Epoch effects, when multiple epochs are present, are sampled as standard normals and centered
to sum to zero in the same way.

Centering avoids confounding with the intercept and makes effects interpretable as deviations
from the global mean.

### Local-level drift (BSTS trend)

We use a Gaussian random walk over the global 1-minute time index, with no epoch resets:

$$
\sigma_{level} \sim \text{HalfNormal}(0.05)
$$

$$
\ell^{raw}_t \sim \text{GaussianRandomWalk}(\sigma_{level}), \qquad
\ell_t = \ell^{raw}_t - \frac{1}{T}\sum_{k=1}^{T}\ell^{raw}_k
$$

In the implementation, the random walk is anchored with an initial distribution
$\ell^{raw}_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,5)$. The mean-centering step removes intercept-trend
confounding by forcing the average latent level over the window to zero.

At 1-minute resolution, a daily random-walk standard deviation is approximately
$\sqrt{1440}\,\sigma_{level}$. With the current prior scale, this is about
$\sqrt{1440} \times 0.05 \approx 1.9\,^\circ\text{C}$.

## Priors

| Parameter | Prior | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ (intercept) | $\mathcal{N}(\bar{y},\, 5)$ | Centred on empirical mean; $\pm 5\,^\circ\text{C}$ covers plausible temperature range |
| $\sigma_{obs}$ | $\text{HalfNormal}(0.2)$ | Tighter measurement-noise prior to reduce tradeoff with latent trend variance |
| $\sigma_{device}$ | $\text{HalfNormal}(0.5)$ | Allows moderate between-device offsets while regularizing toward small calibration differences |
| device offsets $u_d$ | $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{device})$ | Centered hierarchically and then sum-centered to form identifiable relative device effects |
| raw line effects | $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, centered | Only when placement effects are enabled; for two lines the implementation reduces this to one symmetric contrast |
| raw position effects | $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, centered | Only when placement effects are enabled |
| raw epoch effects | $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, centered | Only included when multiple epochs are present |
| $\sigma_{level}$ | $\text{HalfNormal}(0.05)$ | Per-minute innovation scale; implies about 1.9°C day-scale random-walk SD |

# Prior predictive checks

![](results/actual/model/temp_c_prior_predictive.png)


# Fitted model

The model seems well converged with Rhat close to 1:
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("results/actual/model/temp_c_summary.csv")
df.iloc[0:11,:]
```

The device offsets range from -0.11 to +0.13 #TODO
![](results/actual/model/temp_c_device_effect_forest.png)
